text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Yesterday we saw that the congregation; the assembled, called-together people of God carry the Ark of the Covenant on their shoulders through the wilderness of this age. We saw that we carry the presence of God, so that wherever we go the atmosphere changes just as it did wherever the Ark rested. We saw that we carry the fulfilled Law; not condemnation but completed righteousness. And we saw that we carry resurrection life, the testimony of a risen Christ, and the power of the budded rod inside every believer. Today we continue with what remains inside the Ark and with the solemn warning the Ark gives to all who carry it.

Let’s study the golden jar of manna that did not rot in the Ark pointed to Christ as the bread that cannot decay, the eternal nourishment that truly satisfies & the immortal life. As new creation believers, we carry this bread on our shoulders into a world that is desperately, deeply hungry, but feeding on things that rot. Entertainment that leaves people emptier than before. Success that satisfies for a season and then fades.
Religion that gives rules but no life. The world does not know what it is truly hungry for. We carry the answer. We carry Christ, the living bread that came down from heaven.

But we carry this bread in two ways, and the first way is personal. The manna in the wilderness had to be gathered fresh every single morning. If anyone kept it overnight, by morning it had rotted and was full of worms (Exodus 16:20). This was not careless design. God was teaching His people that they could not stockpile His provision.
They had to return to Him every morning. In the same way, we cannot carry the bread of heaven to others if we are not feeding on it ourselves first. The believer who tries to live on last week’s prayer time or bible study, last month’s discourse, or last year’s conviction will find their inner life growing thin and dry. We cannot give what we have not first received. Every morning we must come to the Word, come to prayer, come to Christ and feed on Him before we carry Him to anyone else.

Then, fed and nourished, we carry it to others; through preaching, through teaching, through testimony, through the simple act of sharing the living Christ in everyday conversations. We also carry the promise of the hidden manna. Revelation 2:17 gives this promise to the overcomer: “I will give him some of the hidden manna.” The manna inside the Ark was hidden; the Kohathites carried it on their shoulders every day without ever seeing it. There are depths of Christ not yet fully revealed to anyone. There is more of Him than any of us has yet experienced. We carry that promise on our shoulders into the world, the invitation to discover that Christ is inexhaustible.

On top of the Ark sat the Mercy Seat; the pure gold cover with two cherubim facing each other, their wings spread, looking down at the place where the blood of atonement was sprinkled once a year on the Day of Atonement. Between those cherubim was the glory, the visible presence of Yahweh. And it was there, at the Mercy Seat, that God said:
“There I will meet with you” (Exodus 25:22). Not at a mountain too holy to touch.
Not behind a veil of distance and terror. There, at the place of blood and mercy, man and God would meet.

Romans 3:25 uses the exact same Greek word, “**hilasterion”**; for both the Mercy Seat and for Christ as our propitiation. God presented Christ as a Mercy Seat. He is both the covering and the blood. He is both the meeting place and the sacrifice that makes the meeting possible. Through Him, the way to God has been opened fully and finally.
The veil of the temple was torn from top to bottom when He died (Matthew 27:51); not from the bottom up, as if a man had torn it, but from the top down, because God tore it. The Most Holy Place is open. The Mercy Seat is accessible & God says, Come.

We carry this gospel on our shoulders. We carry the most urgent message in the world, that there is a place where sinful man can truly meet a holy God, not through his own effort, not through religious achievement, not through moral improvement, but through the blood of Christ alone. We carry the news that the Mercy Seat is open and that anyone who comes to God through Christ will not find judgment waiting for them but mercy. This is the thing we carry that the world most desperately needs to hear. The Mercy Seat is not closed. It is open & God says, Come.

The Ark had to be carried with poles inserted through its rings. The Kohathites lifted the poles, not the Ark itself. Their bare hands could never touch the holy chest directly. The poles were not a small detail. They were the instruction of God, and they carried a clear message: you do not handle holy things in your own strength, by your own method, in your own way.

We carry Christ covered, submitted, and under His authority; never in our own name, never by our own cleverness, never through the new carts of human methods that look efficient but bypass the instruction of God. The moment we begin to carry the things of God as though they belong to us, as though our ministry is our own achievement, as though we are sustaining the work of God by our own effort; we are reaching out with bare hands. The Ark must be covered before it is carried, and we will study the meaning of that covering in the days ahead. For now, let us simply carry with reverence, with obedience, and with the deep awareness that what rests on our shoulders does not belong to us.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, you are the Great God, and there is no other. You are high and lifted up, and the train of Your glory fills the temple (Isaiah 6:1). Holy, holy, holy is Yahweh of hosts; the whole earth is full of Your glory. We bow before You as those who carry what is Yours, not what is ours. Thank You for the incorruptible bread; for Christ who never runs out, who never disappoints, who feeds the life completely and forever. Forgive us for feeding on everything else first and coming to You last, for trying to give others what we have not taken time to receive ourselves. Teach us to gather fresh manna every morning before we carry it to anyone else. And thank You for the Mercy Seat; for the torn veil, for the open way, for the blood that did not just cover sin but removed it. Help us carry this gospel without shame, without hesitation, and without softening it. The world needs to know the Mercy Seat is open. Use our shoulders to bring that message to those who have not yet come. And keep us from the bare hands of self-reliance. Let us carry what is Yours, in Your way, under Your covering, for Your glory alone.
